title: Apply: The Battle Over Ratification and the Importance of Compromise
description: Apply your understanding of the battle over ratification and the importance of compromise in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Battle for Ratification
The Battle Over Ratification and the Importance of Compromise
Apply: The Battle Over Ratification and the Importance of Compromise

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Amos Singletary’s speech at the Massachusetts Ratifying Convention.**

We contended with Great Britain—some said for a three-penny duty on tea, but it was not that. It was because they claimed a right to tax us and bind us in all cases whatever. And does not this Constitution do the same? Does it not take away all we have—all our property? Does it not lay all taxes, duties, imposts, and excises? And what more have we to give?

They tell us Congress won't lay [[☃ definition 1]] taxes upon us, but collect all the money they want by impost. I say, there has always been a difficulty about [[☃ definition 2]]. . . . They won't be able to raise money enough by impost, and then they will lay it on the land and take all we have got.

These lawyers, and men of learning, and moneyed men, that talk so finely and gloss over matters so smoothly, to make us poor illiterate people swallow down the pill, expect to get into Congress themselves. They expect to be the managers of this Constitution, and get all the power and all the money into their own hands. 

**Which statement best describes Singletary’s argument in the excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution gives the states the power to resist unnecessary taxes passed by Congress.
The delegates to the Constitutional Convention wrote the Constitution in a way that favored the poor citizens and taxed the rich.
The new Constitution gives Congress too much power and creates a government that too closely resembles a monarchy.
The Articles of Confederation created a government that could not address the needs of the nation as a whole.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Amos Singletary’s speech at the Massachusetts Ratifying Convention.**

We contended with Great Britain—some said for a three-penny duty on tea, but it was not that. It was because they claimed a right to tax us and bind us in all cases whatever. And does not this Constitution do the same? Does it not take away all we have—all our property? Does it not lay all taxes, duties, imposts, and excises? And what more have we to give?

They tell us Congress won't lay [[☃ definition 1]] taxes upon us, but collect all the money they want by impost. I say, there has always been a difficulty about [[☃ definition 2]]. . . . They won't be able to raise money enough by impost, and then they will lay it on the land and take all we have got.

These lawyers, and men of learning, and moneyed men, that talk so finely and gloss over matters so smoothly, to make us poor illiterate people swallow down the pill, expect to get into Congress themselves. They expect to be the managers of this Constitution, and get all the power and all the money into their own hands. 

**Who is Singletary referring to when he discusses “lawyers, and men of learning, and moneyed men”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Federalists
The Anti-Federalists
The Patriots
The Loyalists

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Richard Henry Lee’s 1787 letter to the Governor of Virginia, Edmund Randolph.** 

It has hitherto been supposed a fundamental [[☃ definition 1]], that, in governments rightly balanced, the different branches of legislature should be unconnected, and that the legislative and executive powers should be separate. In the new Constitution, the President and Senate have all the executive, and two thirds of the legislative power. In some weighty instances, (as making all kinds of treaties, which are to be the laws of the land,) they have the whole legislative and executive powers. They, jointly, appoint all officers, civil and military; and they (the Senate) try all impeachments, either of their own members or of the officers appointed by themselves. . . .

It cannot be denied, with truth, that this new Constitution is, in its first principles, highly and dangerously [[☃ definition 2]]; and it is a point agreed, that a government of the few is, of all governments, the worst.

**Which statement best expresses Lee’s criticism of the government set up by the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: relating to a government where power is held by only a few people

choices.content: It did not have enough power to address national problems.
It couldn’t prevent military officers from impeaching the president.
It was too far removed to address the needs of every person.
It was controlled by just a few people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Richard Henry Lee’s 1787 letter to the Governor of Virginia, Edmund Randolph.** 

It has hitherto been supposed a fundamental [[☃ definition 1]], that, in governments rightly balanced, the different branches of legislature should be unconnected, and that the legislative and executive powers should be separate. In the new Constitution, the President and Senate have all the executive, and two thirds of the legislative power. In some weighty instances, (as making all kinds of treaties, which are to be the laws of the land,) they have the whole legislative and executive powers. They, jointly, appoint all officers, civil and military; and they (the Senate) try all impeachments, either of their own members or of the officers appointed by themselves. . . .

It cannot be denied, with truth, that this new Constitution is, in its first principles, highly and dangerously [[☃ definition 2]]; and it is a point agreed, that a government of the few is, of all governments, the worst.

**Which statement best summarizes Lee’s critique of the Constitution  in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: relating to a government where power is held by only a few people

choices.content: The Constitution does not include any checks on the powers of the individual branches of government.
The Constitution creates a system in which power is too concentrated in two parts of the government.
The Constitution does not delegate the powers not listed in the Constitution to the states, as the Articles of Confederation did.
The Constitution forces the different branches of government to work together, slowing down the legislative process.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 letter from James Madison to George Washington. Madison had just arrived at the Confederation Congress to discuss the proposed Constitution.**

I found on my arrival here that certain ideas unfavorable to the Act of the Convention which had created difficulties in that body, had made their way into Congress. . . . It was first urged that as the new Constitution was more than an Alteration of the Articles of Confederation under which Congress acted, and even [[☃ definition 1]] these articles altogether, there was a Constitutional [[☃ definition 2]] in their taking any positive agency in the work. 

The answer given was that the Resolution of Congress in [February] had recommended the Convention as the best mean of obtaining a firm national Government; that as the powers of the Convention were defined by their Commissions in nearly the same terms with the powers of Congress given by the Confederation on the subject of alterations, Congress were not more restrained from [[☃ definition 3]] to the new plan, than the Convention were from proposing it.

**In the excerpt, Madison describes criticism of the Constitution and the Constitutional Convention. Which statement best summarizes that criticism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: completely destroyed the authority of; corrupted

choices.content: The Constitutional Convention had exceeded its powers by proposing a new constitution rather than changing the Articles of Confederation.
The Constitution was ratified by the small group of delegates at the Convention, rather than with Congressional approval or ratification by a popular vote.
The Constitutional Convention was held in secret and its debates were not publicized.
The Constitution did not create a strong central government that was distinctly different from the one created by the Articles of Confederation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 letter from James Madison to George Washington. Madison had just arrived at the Confederation Congress to discuss the proposed Constitution.**

I found on my arrival here that certain ideas unfavorable to the Act of the Convention which had created difficulties in that body, had made their way into Congress. . . . It was first urged that as the new Constitution was more than an Alteration of the Articles of Confederation under which Congress acted, and even [[☃ definition 1]] these articles altogether, there was a Constitutional [[☃ definition 2]] in their taking any positive agency in the work. 

The answer given was that the Resolution of Congress in [February] had recommended the Convention as the best mean of obtaining a firm national Government; that as the powers of the Convention were defined by their Commissions in nearly the same terms with the powers of Congress given by the Confederation on the subject of alterations, Congress were not more restrained from [[☃ definition 3]] to the new plan, than the Convention were from proposing it.

**According to the excerpt, how did delegates at the Constitutional Convention respond to criticism of their work?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: completely destroyed the authority of; corrupted

choices.content: The Constitutional Convention included some of the nation’s brightest political minds, so Congress should accept its recommendations.
The Constitutional Convention’s recommendations changed only the Articles of Confederation and did not exceed its powers.
The Constitutional Convention was the easiest way to create a new government, and Congress gave the convention the powers to alter as they saw fit.
The Constitutional Convention had already accepted the Constitution, so it was the law of the land.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from “A Landholder X,” a Federalist essay by Oliver Ellsworth.**

If there be any one state more interested than the others in the adoption of this [Constitution], it is New-Hampshire. Your local situation which can never be altered is a solemn argument in its favour. Though separated from the government of Britain, at no less price than the blood of your bravest sons, you [[☃ definition 1]]—She is our enemy, and wishes nothing more than your submission to her laws, and to the will of her proud servants. Her force may easily be pointed through your whole territory, and a few regiments would [[☃ definition 2]] banish resistance. New-Hampshire, though growing in population, and among the first states in personal bravery cannot yet stand alone. Should a disunion of the states tempt Britain to make another effort for recovering her former greatness, you will be the first to fall under her sway. 

**What argument does Ellsworth make in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: New Hampshire borders Canadian territory that was governed by Britain at the time the letter was written.
in a way that produces the intended result; effectively

choices.content: If the states do not adopt the Constitution, the country will have no money to pay the military.
If New Hampshire does not agree to adopt the Constitution, it leaves itself vulnerable to British colonization.
If New Hampshire does not ratify the Constitution, it should just return to British rule.
If the states do not reject the new Constitution, the country will break out into a civil war.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from “A Landholder X,” a Federalist essay by Oliver Ellsworth.**

You are the northern barrier of the United States, and by your situation, must first meet any hostile [[☃ definition 1]] from that quarter, designed against any part of them. It is certainly for the interest of a barrier country, to have a general government on such efficient principles, as can point the force of the whole for its relief when attacked—The old Constitution could not do this; [[☃ definition 3]] now under consideration, if accepted, we trust will produce a circulation of riches and the powers of protection, to the most extreme parts of the [[☃ definition 2]]. . . .

**According to Ellsworth, why should New Hampshire adopt the new Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It would have the honor of being the first state to ratify the Constitution.
It would allow states to levy taxes to raise money.
It would allow New Hampshire to expand northward.
It would create a government that can protect the country.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Francis Corbin’s speech at the Virginia Ratification Convention in 1788.**

A [[☃ definition 1]] [[☃ definition 2]] power is absolutely [[☃ definition 3]]. This does not exist under the present articles of Confederation. To [[☃ definition 4]] it with such a power, on its present construction, without any alteration, would be extremely dangerous, and might lead to civil war. Gentlemen must, before this, have been convinced of the necessity of an alteration. Our state-vessel has sprung a leak—We must embark in a new [[☃ definition 5]], or sink into [[☃ definition 6]].

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: using force or authority to require people to do or not do something
total ruin; the place in which one suffers eternal punishment

choices.content: The United States is too weak, so it needs  a written agreement joining all of the states together into one country.
The United States needs a powerful executive whose decisions cannot be questioned; otherwise, the country will break apart.
The United States needs a strong central government, and that is not possible under the Articles of Confederation.
The United States would likely reject any efforts to change the Articles of Confederation, leading to a civil war.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Francis Corbin’s speech at the Virginia Ratification Convention in 1788.**

A [[☃ definition 1]] [[☃ definition 2]] power is absolutely [[☃ definition 3]]. This does not exist under the present articles of Confederation. To [[☃ definition 4]] it with such a power, on its present construction, without any alteration, would be extremely dangerous, and might lead to civil war. Gentlemen must, before this, have been convinced of the necessity of an alteration. Our state-vessel has sprung a leak—We must embark in a new [[☃ definition 5]], or sink into [[☃ definition 6]].

**What action does the author recommend??**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: using force or authority to require people to do or not do something
total ruin; the place in which one suffers eternal punishment

choices.content: Ratifying the Constitution
Preserving the Articles of Confederation
Adopting a Bill of Rights
Fighting a civil war

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article published in Boston’s *Independent Chronicle* in 1788. The author used the [[☃ definition 1]] Samuel.**

But almost every one is possessed with a persuasion, that some farther powers are necessary to be [[☃ definition 2]] the National Head, the Congress; perhaps I am as strongly possessed of that [[☃ definition 3]] as any one; therefore, at first, I felt passive, or rather inclined that this Constitution should be adopted, not considering the nature of it. 

But why should we break such a solemn [[☃ definition 4]], as this Constitution resolves to do? And plunge ourselves into such a [[☃ definition 5]] of innovation, which it is impossible for any one to foresee, or perhaps to guess where it will land us, or what we shall meet by the way? This Constitution does not wear the [[☃ definition 6]] of uniting the nation—but of dividing it. Had we not much better keep on our old ground? The national covenant we are under, solemnly ratified to be [[☃ definition 7]], and amend that: It is, no doubt, as easy to amend that, as it will be to amend the new one. And this I understand, was the sole purpose the federal Convention was appointed for, [[☃ definition 8]] To revise the articles of confederation, not to destroy the covenant.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The ratification of the Constitution will solve the major problems the country has experienced under the Articles of Confederation.
The country should revise the Articles of Confederation, as ratifying the new Constitution will only lead to conflict within the United States.
The country needs to ratify the Constitution immediately; otherwise, the states will rebel and start a civil war.
The colonies need to ratify the Articles of Confederation or they will not be able to win independence from Britain.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best summarizes the message of this political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: An image showing five columns or pillars placed side by side. The pillars are labeled “Del.,” “Pen.,” “N. Jer.,” “Geor.,” and “Con.” A hand coming out of a cloud is placing a sixth pillar, labeled “Mass.,” next to the other side. The text above the pillars reads: “States—like the gen’rous vine supported live, / The strength they gain is from th’embrace they giv[e]. / THE FEDERAL PILLARS.” The text below the pillars reads “United they stand—divided fall.”
options.caption: “The Federal Pillars,” *Massachusetts Centinel,* January 16, 1788. The text reads: “States—like the gen’rous vine supported live, / The strength they gain is from th’embrace they giv[e].”

choices.content: The United States as a country becomes stronger as each state votes to ratify the Constitution.
Citizens’ civil liberties are protected only as each state adds a bill of rights to their state constitutions.
The U.S. economy depends on each state collecting taxes and sending the funds to the federal treasury.
The Constitutional Convention will be successful only if each state sends delegates to represent their interests.
